criteria: The agent has read Section 3.1 completely to understand LLMs' discriminative capability at the decoding level and the motivation for shifting focus from prefill to decoding.
The agent has read Section 3.2 completely to understand the preliminary experiment settings, including the Custom dataset composition, the five evaluated LLMs, and the evaluation strategy.

The agent has read Section 3.3 completely to understand the visualization analysis and the key observation that benign tokens of harmful queries receive higher classifier scores than harmful tokens (distribution differentiation).
The agent has read Section 4 completely to understand the RDS architecture, including step-by-step safe generation and hidden state prediction via EAGLE_Head.

The agent has read Section 5.1 completely to understand the experimental setup, including the three harmful benchmarks, two benign benchmarks, five baselines, and evaluation metric (5 samples per query).
The agent has read Table 1 to check the main experiment results on compliance/refusal ratios across five LLMs and all baselines.
The agent has read Table 2 to check the Just-Eval utility analysis results on Vicuna-13B and Llama2 across five aspects (helpfulness, clarity, factuality, depth, engagement).
The agent has read Table 3 to check the token generation speed (tokens/s) of RDS and baselines across five LLMs.
The agent has read Appendix F and Table 5 to check the classifier AUC evaluation results on Custom, other harmful benchmarks, and Xstest.
The agent has read Appendix C and Table 4 to check the initial defense performance of the five LLMs on the Custom dataset.
The plan includes curation of the Custom dataset with 100 harmful and 100 benign queries sourced from the DRO dataset for classifier training.
The plan includes loading all five evaluated LLMs (Llama-2-chat-7B, Llama-3-8b-Instruct, Qwen2-7B-Instruct, Vicuna-7B-v1.3, Vicuna-13B-v1.3).
The plan includes PCA visualization of hidden states at the top layer of each LLM, sampling the first eight tokens and the last token of decoding outputs.

The plan includes classifier training using Equations 1-4: computing the mean hidden state, applying PCA projection, computing the linear classification score, and minimizing binary cross-entropy loss.
The plan includes using Llama-guard to evaluate compliance of LLM outputs on harmful queries, and string matching with refusal modules to evaluate benign queries.
The plan includes implementation of the step-by-step safe token generation process as formulated in Equation 5, replacing standard sampling with classifier-guided resampling.
The plan includes implementation of the EAGLE_Head module (a fully-connected layer and a decoder layer from the original LLM) for predicting candidate token hidden states as in Equation 10.
The plan includes implementation of the complete RDS Draft_Model inference pipeline as formulated in Equation 11.
The plan includes implementation of all five baselines: safety prompt, Self-Reminder, DRO, Self-Examination, and SafeDecoding.
The plan includes loading all three harmful benchmarks (HEx-PHI, AdvBench, MaliciousInstruct) and two benign benchmarks (Held-out, Xstest).
The plan includes the Just-Eval helpfulness evaluation using GPT-4 as the evaluator, scoring outputs on helpfulness, clarity, factuality, depth, and engagement.
The plan includes the ablation study with the '-w/o SD' variant that removes speculative decoding and uses original multi-layer blocks to compute hidden states.
The plan includes evaluating the classifier's AUC on Custom, other harmful benchmarks (MaliciousInstruct, AdvBench, Held-out), and Xstest.
The plan includes the 5-sample-per-query evaluation strategy for the main experiment, reporting compliance ratio on harmful queries and refusal ratio on benign queries.
The code for loading and preprocessing the Custom dataset (100 harmful + 100 benign queries curated from DRO) has been implemented.
The code for loading all five LLMs (Llama-2-chat-7B, Llama-3-8b-Instruct, Qwen2-7B-Instruct, Vicuna-7B-v1.3, Vicuna-13B-v1.3) and extracting their hidden states at the top layer has been implemented.
The code for computing the mean hidden state u as in Equation 1 (u = (1/n) * sum of h^q for q=1 to n) has been implemented.
The code for computing the PCA projection m_i = V^T * (h_i - u) as in Equation 2, where V contains the m principal components, has been implemented.
The code for computing the linear classification score ŷ_i = W^T * m_i + b as in Equation 3, with trainable parameters W and b, has been implemented.
The code for the binary cross-entropy loss L(y_i, ŷ_i) as in Equation 4, with y_i=1 for harmful queries and y_i=0 for benign queries, has been implemented.
The code for PCA visualization of decoding hidden states (first four principal components) has been implemented, sampling the first eight tokens and the last token of each output.
The code for evaluating LLM outputs using Llama-guard (for harmful query compliance) and refusal string matching (for benign query refusal) has been implemented.
The code for the step-by-step safe generation problem formulation as in Equation 5 (x_i = [x_{i-1}; max(C_i)]; C_i = f(I_i, x_{i-1})) has been implemented.
The code for top-k candidate token extraction via Equation 6 (I_i, V_i = Topk(softmax(l_{i-1})), where l_{i-1} = LM_Head(h_{i-1})) has been implemented.
The code for computing the PCA projection of candidate token hidden states m_k = V^T * (h_i^k - u) as in Equation 7 has been implemented.
The code for computing the harmful score of each candidate token c_k = W^T * m_k + b as in Equation 8 has been implemented.
The code for selecting the safest candidate token via x_i = argmax(C_i) as in Equation 9, where C_i is the set of harmful scores of all candidate tokens, has been implemented.

The EAGLE_Head module (a fully-connected layer plus a decoder layer from the original LLM) has been implemented to predict the hidden state h_i^k from h_{i-1} and the candidate token embedding e_k as in Equation 10.
The complete RDS Draft_Model inference pipeline (x_N = Draft_Model(h_0)) as in Equation 11, combining step-by-step safe generation and EAGLE_Head hidden state prediction, has been implemented.
The '-w/o SD' ablation variant of RDS, which removes the speculative EAGLE_Head and uses standard autoregressive multi-layer blocks to compute hidden states, has been implemented.
The code for the safety prompt baseline (official LLaMA-2 safety system prompt prepended to user queries) has been implemented.
The code for the Self-Reminder baseline, which encapsulates user queries in a system prompt reminding LLMs to respond responsibly, has been implemented.
The code for the DRO baseline, which trains safety prompt embeddings using the distinguishing ability at the prefill level to optimize the moving direction of the input, has been implemented.
The code for the Self-Examination baseline, which checks LLM outputs for harmfulness using the LLM itself with a pre-defined prompt, has been implemented.
The code for the SafeDecoding baseline, which amplifies sampling probabilities of safety-disclaimer tokens learned from a trained expert model, has been implemented.
The code for loading the three harmful benchmarks (HEx-PHI, AdvBench, MaliciousInstruct) and two benign benchmarks (Held-out, Xstest) has been implemented.

The code for the 5-sample-per-query evaluation strategy has been implemented, reporting compliance ratio on harmful queries (via Llama-guard) and refusal ratio on benign queries (via string matching).

The code for Just-Eval helpfulness evaluation using the official Just-Eval prompt and GPT-4 as evaluator, scoring outputs from 1 to 5 on helpfulness, clarity, factuality, depth, and engagement, has been implemented.
The code for measuring token generation speed (tokens per second) of RDS and all baselines across five LLMs has been implemented.
The code for computing classifier AUC on Custom, other harmful benchmarks (MaliciousInstruct, AdvBench, Held-out), and Xstest has been implemented.
The preliminary experiment classifier training on the Custom dataset (5 LLMs) has been executed successfully without errors.
The PCA visualization of decoding hidden states for all five LLMs on the Custom dataset has been executed successfully without errors.

The main security experiments (Table 1) on all five LLMs (Vicuna-7B, Vicuna-13B, Qwen2, Llama2, Llama3), all five baselines, and RDS across all five benchmarks have been executed successfully without errors.
The ablation study experiments (RDS and -w/o SD) on all five LLMs across harmful and benign benchmarks have been executed successfully without errors.
The Just-Eval utility analysis experiments on Vicuna-13B and Llama2 with DRO, Self-Examination, SafeDecoding, and RDS have been executed successfully without errors.
The token generation speed measurement experiments on all five LLMs with all defense methods have been executed successfully without errors.
The classifier AUC evaluation on all five LLMs across Custom, other harmful benchmarks, and Xstest has been executed successfully without errors.

The execution outcomes of the PCA visualization match Figure 2: LLMs can discern the harmfulness of inputs, and benign tokens of harmful queries receive higher harmful classifier scores than actually harmful tokens (distribution differentiation rather than hard classification).
The execution outcomes match Table 1: RDS achieves lower compliance ratios on harmful queries compared to all baselines for Vicuna-7B (5.3 vs. 42.3 for no defense) and other models.
The execution outcomes match Table 1: RDS achieves significantly lower refusal ratios on benign queries compared to SafeDecoding and DRO (e.g., 6.0 RDS vs. 38.0 SafeDecoding on Vicuna-13B).
The execution outcomes match Table 1: the '-w/o SD' ablation variant achieves comparable or slightly better safety performance than full RDS but at the cost of inference speed.

The execution outcomes match Table 1: Self-Examination causes catastrophic refusal on Llama2, achieving 100.0 refusal on both Held-out and Xstest, while RDS maintains 32.5 refusal (matching no defense).
The execution outcomes match Table 2: RDS maintains utility comparable to no-defense on Vicuna-13B (average 4.38 vs. 4.49) while other methods like DRO degrade utility (3.99).
The execution outcomes match Table 2: RDS substantially outperforms Self-Examination on Llama2 in utility (average 4.42 vs. 2.06), confirming RDS avoids catastrophic over-refusal.

The execution outcomes match Table 3: RDS with speculative decoding achieves 2.12x to 3.09x faster token generation than baselines (e.g., 73.17 tokens/s for Vicuna-7B vs. ~31-43 tokens/s for baselines).

The execution outcomes match Table 3: RDS '-w/o SD' has significantly lower inference speed than full RDS (e.g., 21.25 vs. 73.17 tokens/s for Vicuna-7B), confirming the contribution of speculative decoding.
The execution outcomes match Table 5: the classifier achieves AUC ~1.0 on Custom (training data) and ~0.99-1.0 on other harmful benchmarks across all five LLMs, demonstrating strong transferability.

The execution outcomes match Table 5: the classifier achieves lower AUC on Xstest (0.65-0.89 across models) compared to other benchmarks, consistent with the observation that Xstest contains contextually ambiguous queries.

The execution outcomes match Table 4: the initial defense performance of the five LLMs on Custom (e.g., Vicuna-7B: 5 compliance on harmful, 3 refusal on benign; Vicuna-13B: 0/0; Llama-2: 0/0; Llama3: 9/0; Qwen2: 0/0).
